query: What would you hear if you were travelling at the speed of sound towards/away from something making noise? What would you hear if you spoke?

physics - What would sound sound like at the speed of sound? - Sound Design Stack Exchange big brain on @Colin. Nice explanation! ΓÇô Steve Urban Jul 29 '10 at 14:49 This is the extreme limit of the doppler effect. You hear "sound" as the changes in pressure of the sound wave. If you are travelling along with the sound wave, then you won't get any changes in pressure so you won't hear it. One way to conceptualize it is that sound waves are like the grooves in a record. The normal situation is you (the needle) are standing still and the sound wave (record) travels past you at a fixed rate (the speed of sound in  Answer:

baseline.final_answer: If you were traveling at the speed of sound towards a sound source, you would not hear it because you would be moving along with the sound wave, experiencing no change in pressure. Similarly, if you were moving away from a sound source at the speed of sound, sounds from behind you could not catch up, rendering them inaudible.

evaluation.feedback: The answer contains factual claims about sound perception at the speed of sound but lacks any citations to support these claims. The provided documents do not contain the answer.
